editorial_contributions.text:
Summary: \textit{A. S. Kechris} et al. [Geom. Funct. Anal. 15, No. 1, 106--189 (2005; Zbl 1084.54014)] provided a powerful tool to compute an invariant of topological groups known as the universal minimal flow, immediately leading to an explicit representation of this invariant in many concrete cases. More recently, the framework was generalized allowing for further applications, and the purpose of this paper is to apply these new methods in the context of homogeneous directed graphs.
In this paper, we show that the age of any homogeneous directed graph allows a Ramsey precompact expansion. Moreover, we verify the relative expansion properties and consequently describe the respective universal minimal flows.

msc.text: Dynamical systems involving transformations and group actions with special properties (minimality, distality, proximality, expansivity, etc.)

Let A ‚ąą Age(IŌČ[C3]), then any expansion A‚ąóof A will embed in any expansion of {(i, j) : i ‚ąą coll(A), j ‚ąą [3]}. 8Complete n-partite directed graphs For n 6 ŌČ, by a complete n-partite directed graph we mean a directed graph such that the non-edge relation ‚ä• is an equivalence relation with at most n many classes. For each n, the class of all such graphs is a Fra¬®ńĪss¬īe class. The corresponding limit is denoted n ‚ąó IŌČ, and consists of n many disjoint copies of IŌČbetween which edges are distributed in a complete and random way. For n ‚ąą N we construct the expansion n ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąóby adding a convex linear ordering together with n many unary relations Pin‚ąóIŌČ‚ąócorresponding to the parts of n ‚ąó IŌČand so that P0n‚ąóIŌČ‚ąó<n‚ąóIŌČ‚ąó¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ <n‚ąóIŌČ‚ąóPn‚ąí1n‚ąóIŌČ‚ąó. For n = ŌČ, construct ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąóby simply adding to ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČa convex linear ordering that orders each part in a dense linear order, as well as the set of parts in a dense linear order; note that we do not add any unary predicates in this case. Those expansions are precompact, and we show that their ages have the Ramsey property and show the appropriate expansion properties. If B is a complete n-partite directed graph, say that a subgraph is transversal when it has exactly one point in each ‚ä• equivalence class of B. the electronic journal of combinatorics 21(4) (2014), #P4.4216 Remark 8.1. It came to the attention of the authors that there is a simpler proof of the lemmas below, based on the result in [S13-2]: One takes the output of the result of [S13-2] and prunes it, arriving at an element of the age of n ‚ąó IŌČ. Though, we include the direct proof based on the partite construction, we will revisit the result of [S13-2] in section 9. Lemma 8.2. Let n, r ‚ąą N be positive and suppose B is a finite substructure of n ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąówith exactly n parts. Suppose A is an n-partite directed subgraph of B which is transversal. Then there exists a finite n-partite graph C such that C ‚Üí (B)Ar. Proof. This is identical to the proof of the partite lemma in [NR89]. Suppose now that A is a directed subgraph of B, and that the parts of B are written Yiwith i ‚ąą [n]. Define the trace of A (in B) to be tr(A) = {i : A ‚ą© Yi6= ‚ąÖ}. For x ‚ąą B, define the trace of x to be tr(x) = tr({x}), thus x ‚ąą Ytr(x). We begin with the following special case of the Ramsey property. Lemma 8.3. Let n, r ‚ąą N with r > 0, suppose A 6 B are finite substructure of n ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąó with exactly n parts each. Then there exists a finite C ‚äÜ n ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąósuch that C ‚Üí (B)Ar. Proof. What follows is a modification of the partite construction in [NR89]. Let A and B be as above, and denote by Yi, with i < n, the parts of B. Set a = |A|, b = |B|, ai= |A ‚ą© Yi|, and bi= |B ‚ą© Yi| for i < n. By Lemma 3.2, there exist disjoint Ci‚äÜ N with i < n, such that (C0, . . . , Cn‚ąí1) ‚Üí (b0, . . . , bn‚ąí1)(ar0,...,an‚ąí1). We can assume that C0= {1, . . . , |C0|} and in general, Ci= {max(Ci‚ąí1) + 1, . . . , max(Ci‚ąí1) + |Ci|}. Set p = max(Cn‚ąí1). Enumerate C0, . . . , Cn‚ąí1 = {N1, . . . , NőĪ}, a0, . . . , an‚ąí1 and C0, . . . , Cn‚ąí1 = {M1, . . . , Mő≤}. b0, . . . , bn‚ąí1 Now for every j ‚ąą [ő≤], Mjis a disjoint union of Ajk‚Äôs such that Ajk= Mj‚ą© Ck, for every k < n. To complete our set of edges, for every i < i0and j < j0, if i ‚ąą Ajkand i0‚ąą Ajk00 where k < k0, then we add ((i, j), (i0, j0)). For every k ‚ąą [n], PkQ0is the disjoint union of Bj Pk‚Äôs. Moreover, we set each non-structural part to be Mi= {(i, j) : j ‚ąą [ő≤]} for i ‚ąą [p]. Finally, add a linear ordering (convex with respect to both partitions) that extends the linear orderings that already exist on each copy of B: If j < j0, then set (i, j) <Q0(i, j0). If i < i0, j 6= j0, then set (i, j) <Q0(i0, j0). The resulting structure is Q0. Next, we construct a sequence Q1, . . ., QőĪinductively using lemma 8.3, as in [NR89]. To construct Qifrom Qi‚ąí1, we first consider the directed subgraph of Qi‚ąí1whose trace is Ni‚ąí1with respect to the non-structural partition, call it Hi‚ąí1. Applying lemma 8.3, we get an a-partite Gi‚ąí1such that Gi‚ąí1‚Üí (Hi‚ąí1)Ar. Subsequently, we expand each copy of Hi‚ąí1to a copy of Qi‚ąí1, and add a convex (with respect to the structural partition) linear ordering that extends all the linear orderings that already exist on each copy of Qi‚ąí1. We call the result Qi. Following this procedure, the directed graph induced by the structure QőĪis not complete with respect to the structural partition, but it can be made complete by adding new edges. Let C be any structure obtained that way. We claim that C is as required. Let Ōá :AC‚Üí [r] be any colouring. Then there will be Q0‚ąąQC0 such that the colouring of each copy of A depends only on its trace with respect to the non-structural partition. The conclusion follows from the construction of Q0now using routine arguments. We are now ready to prove the general result. Theorem 8.4. The age of n ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąóhas the Ramsey property for each n ‚ąą N. Proof. Let k 6 n, r ‚ąą N with r > 0, B a finite substructure of n ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąówith exactly n parts, and A is a substructure of B with k parts. Then we will show that there exists a finite C ‚äÜ n ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąówith n parts such that C ‚Üí (B)Ar. Note that because of the unary relations that are interpreted as the parts of n ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąó, all copies of A in B have same trace ŌĄ . Let ¬ĮB be the substructure of B induced by the points v ‚ąą B with tr(v) ‚ąą ŌĄ . Apply Lemma 8.3 to find ¬ĮC such that ¬ĮC ‚Üí ( ¬ĮB)Ar. ŌÉ be the trace of B. Now extend each copy of ¬ĮB in ¬ĮC to a copy of ¬ĮB, via amalgamation. Set C to be the result of this extension with appropriate edges and linear ordering added. This result allows us to show the same is true for the infinite partite structure. the electronic journal of combinatorics 21(4) (2014), #P4.4218 Theorem 8.5. The age of ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąóhas the Ramsey property. Proof. Let k 6 n, r ‚ąą N with r > 0, B a finite substructure of ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąówith n parts, and A a substructure of B with k parts. Expand for the moment ŌČ ‚ąóIŌČ‚ąówith unary predicates corresponding to its parts, calling the resulting structure ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąó‚ąó. For each i 6 l consider a copy Biof B with trace ŌĄiin ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąóso that the Bi‚Äôs are pairwise disjoint. Set ¬ĮB to be the substructure of ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąósupported by the union of these Bi‚Äôs. Note that all copies of A with fixed trace in ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąóinduce isomorphic structures in ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąó‚ąó. Using Theorem 8.4, we can find a finite substructure C of ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąósuch that every r-colouring Ōá ofAC, there exists a copy ¬ĮB0‚ąąCB¬Į such that Ōá restricted toB¬ĮA0 depends only on the trace. To complete the proof, define ¬ĮŌá :Nk ‚Üí [r] by setting ¬ĮŌá(ŌÉ) to be the colour of any copy of A in ¬ĮB0with trace ŌÉ; this is well defined by construction. By the choice of N , there is a ¬ĮŌá-monochromatic ŌĄi0, and by construction, there is B0‚ąąB¬Į0 with trace ŌĄ Bi0, which completes the proof. We now more to the expansion property, and note that the proof of the theorem below follows the template found in the proof of Lemma 5.2 in [N96] and the proofs of the expansion properties found in [S13-1]. Theorem 8.6. The age of ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąósatisfies the expansion property with respect to the age of ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ. Proof. Let A ‚ąą Age(ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ), and fix an arbitrary a convex linear ordering < on A. We will produce a structure B ‚ąą Age(ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ) such that A‚ąóembeds in any expansion of B. Consider the following structures: ‚ÄĘ E1is the linearly ordered directed graph with universe {a, b} and the directed edge (a, b) such that a < b. ‚ÄĘ E2is the linearly ordered directed graph with universe {a, b} and no edges such that a < b. Set <revto be the same as < but with the ordering on each part (i.e., the ordering on non-edges) reversed. Set >revto be the same as > but with the ordering on each part reversed. Now find A1‚ąą Age(ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąó) so that (A, <), (A, >), (A, <rev) and (A, >rev) embed in A1, and similarly find A2‚ąą Age(ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąó) so that A1, E1and E2all embed in A2. Since Age(ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąó) satisfies the Ramsey property, we can find Ci‚ąą Age(ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ‚ąó) so that C1‚Üí (A2)E21and C2‚Üí (C1)E22. Let <0be the ordering on C2, and ‚Čļ be any convex linear ordering on C2, and consider the following colouring on ordered pairs in the universe of C2: ( 0if the two orders <0and ‚Čļ agree on {u, v}. Ōá((u, v)) = 1otherwise the electronic journal of combinatorics 21(4) (2014), #P4.4219 This colouring induces colourings Ōáion the copies of Eiin C2for each i. By the above Ramsey construction, there is B ‚ąąCA2 which is monochromatic with respect to each Ōái. 2 In other words, we have the following possibilities: 1. The ordering ‚Čļ on B agrees with <0. 2. The ordering ‚Čļ on agrees with <0on edges and goes in the opposite direction on non-edges. 3. The ordering ‚Čļ on B agrees with <0on non-edges and goes in the opposite direction on edges. 4. The ordering ‚Čļ on B goes in the opposite direction. Now, since A1embeds in B, the structure (A, <) must embed in B with the order <0 replaced by ‚Čļ and the proof is complete. Theorem 8.7. Add the corresponding imaginary points t and t0. For each transversal pair of {u, v, x, y}, complete it into a quadruple using {t, t0} where the parity constraint is satisfied. Identifying ES[n,k]‚ąó with its characteristic function, this is equivalent to saying some sum equals 0 modulo 2. For example, for {u, x}: ES[n,k]‚ąó(u, x) + ES[n,k]‚ąó(u, t0) + ES[n,k]‚ąó(t, x) + ES[n,k]‚ąó(t, t0) = 0. Suppose F is one of the homogeneous directed graphs and F‚ąó= (F,‚ąíR‚Üí‚ąó) is its precompact expansion which we have constructed. The age of F‚ąósatisfies the Ramsey property as well as the respective expansion property and the electronic journal of combinatorics 21(4) (2014), #P4.4228 consists of rigid elements. Consequently, its automorphism group is extremely amenable. Let G be the automorphism group of F. Relations giving the expanded tournament T‚ąóthe Ramsey property and the expansion property; <‚ąó(convex linear ordering on F‚ąó) n ‚ąó IŌČP1F‚ąó, . . . , PnF‚ąó(unary relations distinguishing each part of n ‚ąó In), and <‚ąó (convex linear ordering on F‚ąó) ŌČ ‚ąó IŌČ<‚ąó(convex linear ordering on F‚ąó) semigeneric<‚ąó(convex linear ordering on F‚ąó), and RF‚ąó(sub-partitioning binary relation) Note that the relations including the linear orders in the above table arise from taking the Fra¬®ńĪss¬īe limits of our expanded classes. Note that the semigeneric case is the only one where a special binary relation was used (in addition to a linear order). Completing the list of Ramsey precompact expansions of homogeneous structures and subsequently computing their universal minimal flows is an ever continuing undertaking. For example, we have examples of homogeneous metric spaces whose Ramsey precompact expansions are still unknown. In particular from the work of Cherlin in [C13], and related more closely to the subject of this paper, are classes of metrically homogeneous graphs, i.e., ones that are homogeneous with respect to the graph metric. the electronic journal of combinatorics 21(4) (2014), #P4.4229 Acknowledgements This project was finished while both of us, Claude Laflamme and Lionel Nguyen Van Th¬īe, were attending the thematic program Universality and Homogeneity at the Hausdorff Research Institute for Mathematics in Bonn.
